questions.prompt: Glean describes three agent run-modes. Which list matches them?
options: Run on Glean, call via the API, expose as an MCP server
Run on-premise, run in the browser, run on a phone
Free tier, pro tier, enterprise tier
Search, summarize, and translate
questions.explanation: An agent can be built and run on Glean, invoked from your code via the API, or exposed to other hosts over MCP — same graph, different trust boundaries.

questions.prompt: When a host connects to Glean's MCP server with a scoped token, what happens to access?
options: The host gets admin access to the whole graph
Retrieval stays permissions-aware — the token resolves to a user and returns only what that user may see
All permission checks are bypassed for speed
The host can only read public documents
questions.explanation: The MCP endpoint relays access; it does not widen it. The token acts as a user, so retrieval remains scoped to that identity.

questions.prompt: In a guarded multi-tool agent loop, what is the job of the pre-action gate?
options: To speed up retrieval
To block the final, irreversible action unless the user's role permits it
To rephrase the user's query
To cache search results
questions.explanation: The pre-action gate is the last line before the agent touches the world — a MANAGE control that stops an unpermitted action.

questions.prompt: Across CrewAI, Google ADK, LangGraph, and the OpenAI Agents SDK, what does every Glean tool have in common?
options: They all require Rego policies
They each carry a token that resolves to a user, so the agent can only retrieve what that user is allowed to see
They all run only inside Glean's platform
They disable audit logging
questions.explanation: The framework changes, but the trust boundary does not: the tool's token is per-user, so retrieval stays permissions-aware everywhere.

questions.prompt: Which statement about this curriculum is accurate?
options: The 200–500 level ladder is an official Glean certification
The NIST AI RMF crosswalk is a documented Glean mapping
Both the level ladder and the NIST AI RMF mapping are our instructional framing, not official Glean artifacts
Glean issues a credential when you finish Week 8
questions.explanation: The level ladder and the RMF crosswalk are instructional synthesis we use to organize and reason about the material — neither is an official Glean certification or a Glean-published mapping.
